title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: curveR ecosystem contains three packages share common data contract defined curveRcore. curveRcore provides data structures, preprocessing pipeline (preprocess_standards()), settings constructors (new_antigen_constraints(), new_study_params(), new_fit_options()) used identically fitting engines. curveRbayes (package) fits Bayesian hierarchical dose‚Äďresponse models via Stan produces posterior concentration estimates full uncertainty propagation. curveRfreq provides frequentist (NLS/NLME) alternatives settings speed regulatory precedent primary concern; head--head comparison engines ecosystem comparison vignette.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: bead_assay_example ships curveRbayes contains simulated multi-plate bead-based immunoassay data two antigens (alpha, beta), measured three plates ‚ÄĒ six curve_id values total. key design principle curve_id sole foreign key uniquely identifying one calibration curve ‚ÄĒ encoding antigen, study, experiment, plate together. curve_id_lookup decodes human-readable labels, fitting, filtering, indexing uses integer curve_id alone. raw $standards data frame carries dilution mfi. Neither concentration column log-transformed responses exist yet ‚ÄĒ created preprocessing.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: preprocess_standards() applies four steps fixed canonical order, identically curveRbayes curveRfreq: Concentration computation ‚ÄĒ (1 / dilution) √ó std_curve_conc, written concentration column, optionally log10-transformed. Prozone correction ‚ÄĒ compresses post-peak hook-effect deflections. Blank handling ‚ÄĒ one five strategies; "ignored" default. Response log-transform ‚ÄĒ log10(mfi), non-positive values floored adaptively transformation. preprocessing, concentration holds log10(AU/mL) mfi holds log10(MFI) ‚ÄĒ exact values Stan receives, confirmed build_stan_data() pulls standards$concentration standards[[response_variable]] directly. {r plot-preprocessed,     fig.cap="Preprocessed calibration standards antigen alpha (curve_id 1‚Äď3). axes log10-transformed ‚ÄĒ exact values passed Stan. curve_id one plate; inter-plate differences MFI level curve shape variability hierarchical model handles."} standards_preprocessed |>   dplyr::filter(curve_id %% 1:3) |>   ggplot(aes(x = concentration, y = mfi,              colour = factor(curve_id), group = curve_id)) +   geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x      = "log\u2081\u2080(concentration)  [AU/mL]",     y      = "log\u2081\u2080(MFI)",     colour = "curve_id",     title  = "Preprocessed standards ‚ÄĒ antigen alpha"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") three curves share biological dose‚Äďresponse differ absolute MFI offset inflection point position ‚ÄĒ precisely inter-plate variability hierarchical model designed handle.
code: # CONC in bead_assay_example runs 0.001‚Äď30 AU/mL; dilution = 1/CONC, # so std_curve_conc = 1/min(dilution) = max(CONC) = 30. antigen_settings <- new_antigen_constraints(   antigen        = "alpha",   std_curve_conc = 30,   l_asy_method   = "default",   pcov_threshold = 20 )  study_params <- new_study_params(   is_log_response    = TRUE,   is_log_independent = TRUE,   apply_prozone      = TRUE,   blank_option       = "ignored" ) # Preprocess all six curve_ids (both antigens, all plates) prep <- preprocess_standards(   data                 = bead_assay_example$standards,   antigen_settings     = antigen_settings,   response_variable    = bead_assay_example$response_var,   independent_variable = bead_assay_example$indep_var,   is_log_response      = study_params$is_log_response,   blank_data           = bead_assay_example$blanks,   blank_option         = study_params$blank_option,   is_log_independent   = study_params$is_log_independent,   apply_prozone        = study_params$apply_prozone )  standards_preprocessed <- prep$data  # After preprocessing: concentration = log10(AU/mL), mfi = log10(MFI) head(standards_preprocessed[, c("curve_id", "dilution",                                  "concentration", "mfi")], 4) #>   curve_id   dilution concentration      mfi #> 1        1 1000.00000   -1.52287875 2.039017 #> 2        1  333.33333   -1.04575749 2.500922 #> 3        1  100.00000   -0.52287875 3.054230 #> 4        1   33.33333   -0.04575749 3.618686

text: Quick convergence summary ‚ÄĒ curve_id 1.
code: # 1. Quick convergence summary across all models (curve_id 1) lapply(ensemble, function(m) {   fs <- m$fit_stats   tibble::tibble(     n_divergent    = fs$n_divergent,     n_max_treedepth = fs$n_max_treedepth,     mean_ebfmi     = round(mean(fs$ebfmi, na.rm = TRUE), 3)   ) }) |>   dplyr::bind_rows(.id = "model") |>   knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Quick convergence summary ‚ÄĒ curve_id 1.") # 2. Population inflection point: posterior mean and 95% CI mu_c_draws <- as.numeric(draws_4pl[["mu_c"]]) cat(sprintf(   "mu_c:  mean = %.3f  95%% CI [%.3f, %.3f]  (log10 AU/mL)\n",   mean(mu_c_draws),   quantile(mu_c_draws, 0.025),   quantile(mu_c_draws, 0.975) )) #> mu_c:  mean = 0.073  95% CI [-0.560, 0.760]  (log10 AU/mL)  # 3. title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: pooled model treats plates single experiment, conflating within-plate -plate variance producing overconfident predictions. separate-plate model fits plate independently, discarding shared biology producing underconfident estimates single plate calibrators. Bayesian hierarchical model occupies optimal middle ground: plate-level parameters exchangeable draws common population distribution, sharing information across plates still adapting plate‚Äôs data.

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: model families share hierarchical skeleton. Using 4PL logistic (logistic4) reference: yi,k=Ak+Dk‚ąíAk1+(xiCk)Bk+őĶi,k y_{,k} \;=\; A_k + \frac{D_k - A_k}{1 + \left(\dfrac{x_i}{C_k}\right)^{B_k}} \;+\; \varepsilon_{,k} kk indexes curve_id, xix_i log10-concentration standard ii (concentration column preprocessing), őĶi,k\varepsilon_{,k} observation noise. logistic5 loglogistic5 add per-curve_id asymmetry parameter GkG_k population mean SD. gompertz4 replaces logistic link Gompertz function keeps four-parameter hierarchy. log‚Äďlog scale (is_log_independent = TRUE, is_log_response = TRUE), loglogistic4 mathematically equivalent logistic4. fit_calibration_bayes() therefore automatically drops loglogistic4 candidate set transformations active.

text: centred parameterisation őłk‚ąľūĚí©(őľ,ŌÉ)\theta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma) creates funnel geometry: ŌÉ\sigma small sampler must simultaneously explore tight neck wide wings, producing divergent transitions. curveRbayes uses non-centred parameterisation (NCP) throughout: őłk=őľőł+ŌÉőł‚čÖzk,zk‚ąľūĚí©(0,1) \theta_k \;=\; \mu_\theta + \sigma_\theta \cdot z_k, \qquad z_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) sampler explores standardised offset zkz_k ‚ÄĒ always flat ūĚí©(0,1)\mathcal{N}(0,1) geometry ‚ÄĒ global hyperparameters (őľőł,ŌÉőł)(\mu_\theta, \sigma_\theta) independently. likelihood evaluation recombined őłk\theta_k. transformation eliminates funnel primary reason Stan achieves near-zero divergences models. parameters transformed parameters blocks logistic4 illustrate pattern: Note b exponentiated remains strictly positive. inflection point c_par asymptotes left fitting scale (already log10 preprocessing).
code: parameters {   // population hyperparameters   real mu_a;   real mu_log_b;   real mu_c;          // inflection on the fitting scale   real mu_d;    // population SDs ‚ÄĒ Half-Normal keeps these positive   real<lower=0> sigma_a;   real<lower=0> sigma_log_b;   real<lower=0> sigma_c;   real<lower=0> sigma_d;    // non-centred offsets: one per curve_id (plate)   vector[N_plates] raw_a;   vector[N_plates] raw_log_b;   vector[N_plates] raw_c;   vector[N_plates] raw_d;    // observation noise (pooled across all curve_ids)   real<lower=0> sigma_obs;   // homoscedastic SD   real<lower=2> nu;           // Student-t degrees of freedom   real<lower=0> sigma_blank;    // heteroscedastic noise ‚ÄĒ always estimated; active in likelihood   // only when use_heteroscedastic_noise = 1   real log_sigma0;   real log_sigma_slope; }  transformed parameters {   // plate-level parameters reconstructed from NCP offsets   vector[N_plates] a     = mu_a     + sigma_a     * raw_a;   vector[N_plates] b     = exp(mu_log_b + sigma_log_b * raw_log_b);   vector[N_plates] c_par = mu_c     + sigma_c     * raw_c;   vector[N_plates] d     = mu_d     + sigma_d     * raw_d; }

LOO-CV estimates expected log predictive density (ELPD) held-observation without refitting: elpdŐāloo=‚ąĎ=1Nlogp(yi‚ą£y‚ąí) \widehat{\text{elpd}}_\text{loo} \;=\; \sum_{=1}^{N} \log p(y_i \mid y_{-}) Pareto-kk diagnostic flags observations whose importance weights unreliable: {r pareto-k,     fig.cap="Pareto-k diagnostic logistic4. Points k = 0.7 (dashed line) flag high-leverage calibrators disproportionately influence fit."} (!.null(loo_list[["logistic4"]])) {   plot(loo_list[["logistic4"]],        diagnostic   = "k",        label_points = TRUE,        main         = "Pareto-k ‚ÄĒ logistic4")   abline(h = 0.7, lty = 2, col = "firebrick") }

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: Rather selecting single best model, curveRbayes computes Bayesian stacking weights (Yao et al. 2018) ‚ÄĒ weight vector ūĚźį\mathbf{w} maximises stacked ELPD: ūĚźįŐā=argmaxūĚźį‚ąĎ=1Nlog‚ąĎk=1Kwkp(yi‚ą£y‚ąí,‚Ą≥k),wk‚Č•0,‚ąĎkwk=1 \hat{\mathbf{w}} \;=\; \arg\max_{\mathbf{w}} \sum_{=1}^{N} \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \, p(y_i \mid y_{-}, \mathcal{M}_k), \quad w_k \ge 0,\; \textstyle\sum_k w_k = 1 Stacking preferable winner-takes-model selection calibrated model misspecification: even single candidate true data-generating process, stacked ensemble achieves best achievable predictive accuracy candidate set. Bayesian stacking weights across candidate models. {r stacking-bar,     fig.cap="Stacking weights. single dominant weight (> 0.9) supports using model alone; mixed weights suggest genuine model uncertainty."} (!.null(stacking_weights)) {   tibble::enframe(stacking_weights, name = "model", value = "weight") |>     ggplot(aes(x = reorder(model, -weight), y = weight, fill = model)) +     geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1),                        labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +     labs(x = "Model", y = "Stacking weight",          title = "Bayesian stacking weights") +     theme_minimal(base_size = 12) }

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Calibration with curveRbayes
text: predict_grid_bayes() supports two modes precision profiling, selected fitting time via use_heteroscedastic_noise. Mode 0 ‚ÄĒ Posterior-predictive precision (default, use_heteroscedastic_noise = FALSE) constant sigma_obs drawn posterior step. precision profile measures accurately curve back-calculate concentration instrument produced response drawn homoscedastic Student-t distribution. valid Bayesian precision measure noise injected Step 3 (see Section @ref(cdan-procedure)) vary signal level ‚ÄĒ profile shape dominated inverse-curve geometry (steeply inv(y) magnifies small errors) rather concentration-dependent noise scaling. Mode 1 ‚ÄĒ CDAN precision (use_heteroscedastic_noise = TRUE) Stan models additionally estimate two noise parameters ‚ÄĒ log_sigma0 (intercept) log_sigma_slope (slope) ‚ÄĒ define power--mean variance function: ŌÉi=exp(logŌÉ0(s)+logŌÉslope(s)‚čÖlog|őľi|) \sigma_i = \exp\!\left(\log\sigma_0^{(s)}   + \log\sigma_\text{slope}^{(s)} \cdot \log\!\left|\mu_i\right|\right) variance function v(m,őł)=őł1mőł2v(m, \theta) = \theta_1 m^{\theta_2} O‚ÄôMalley (2008) ¬ß2, defining feature Concentration Distribution Assay Noise (CDAN) precision profile. ŌÉi\sigma_i scaling signal level, precision profile captures inverse-curve geometry fact instrument noise larger high response values ‚ÄĒ giving profile characteristic U-shape precision degrades near asymptotes different reasons. noise_mode column every grid data frame records path active ("heteroscedastic" "homoscedastic"), making easy verify plots downstream comparisons. compare modes, fit twice:

Near inflection point curve steep ‚ÄĒ small response uncertainty maps small concentration uncertainty. code:
dplyr::glimpse(cr1$grid) #> Rows: 200 #> Columns: 13 #> $ log10_concentration     <dbl> -4.000000, -3.972477, -3.944954, -3.917430, -3‚Ä¶ #> $ concentration           <dbl> 0.0001000000, 0.0001065426, 0.0001135132, 0.00‚Ä¶ #> $ x_fit                   <dbl> -4.000000, -3.972477, -3.944954, -3.917430, -3‚Ä¶ #> $ predicted_response      <dbl> 1.398742, 1.400610, 1.402525, 1.404490, 1.4065‚Ä¶ #> $ ci_lower                <dbl> 1.171737, 1.174983, 1.178430, 1.181949, 1.1855‚Ä¶ #> $ ci_upper                <dbl> 1.784976, 1.785086, 1.785201, 1.785323, 1.7854‚Ä¶ #> $ predicted_concentration <dbl> -3.880520, -3.862948, -3.856497, -3.881174, -3‚Ä¶ #> $ se_concentration        <dbl> 0.6794048, 0.6907654, 0.8896030, 0.9149750, 0.‚Ä¶ #> $ pcov                    <dbl> 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.00‚Ä¶ #> $ pcov_rmse               <dbl> 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.0000, 150.00‚Ä¶ #> $ pcov_pass               <lgl> FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALS‚Ä¶ #> $ noise_mode              <chr> "homoscedastic", "homoscedastic", "homoscedast‚Ä¶ #> $ d2y_dx2                 <dbl> NA, 0.06311380, 0.06477741, 0.06648632, 0.0682‚Ä¶ # noise_mode column records which precision profile mode was used unique(cr1$grid$noise_mode) #> [1] "homoscedastic" ---  # The three-step noise injection procedure {#cdan-procedure}  `predict_grid_bayes()` constructs the precision profile at each grid concentration $x^*$ via a three-step procedure that propagates both curve-parameter uncertainty and instrument measurement noise. The noise model used in Step 3 depends on `use_heteroscedastic_noise`.  ## Step 1 ‚ÄĒ draw posterior curve parameters {#step1}  For each of the $S$ posterior samples, retrieve the full set of per-`curve_id` parameters:  $$  oldsymbol{	heta}_k^{(s)} = \left\{A_k^{(s)},\; B_k^{(s)},\; C_k^{(s)},\; D_k^{(s)}ight\} $$  This captures **parameter uncertainty**: how much the curve shape varies across the posterior.  ## Step 2 ‚ÄĒ evaluate the forward model {#step2}  For each sample $s$ and `curve_id` $k$, evaluate the forward model at $x^*$ (log10-concentration):  $$ 	ilde{y}^{(s,k)} = A_k^{(s)} +  rac{D_k^{(s)} - A_k^{(s)}}        {1 + \left(x^* / C_k^{(s)}ight)^{B_k^{(s)}}} $$  (For `gompertz4` the Gompertz link replaces the logistic fraction; for `logistic5`/`loglogistic5` the asymmetry parameter $G_k^{(s)}$ modifies the denominator.)  ## Step 3 ‚ÄĒ inject noise and invert {#step3}  **This step is the defining feature of the precision profile and is where the two noise modes diverge.**  To each forward-predicted response $	ilde{y}^{(s,k)}$, add a noise draw scaled by $\sigma_i^{(s)}$:  $$ y^{*(s,k)} = 	ilde{y}^{(s,k)}   + \varepsilon^{(s)}, \qquad \varepsilon^{(s)} \sim t_{
u^{(s)}}\!\left(0,\; \sigma_i^{(s)}ight) $$  where $\sigma_i^{(s)}$ is determined by the noise mode:  $$ \sigma_i^{(s)} =  egin{cases} \sigma_	ext{obs}^{(s)}   & 	ext{if } 	exttt{use\_heteroscedastic\_noise = FALSE}   \quad	ext{(homoscedastic)} \[6pt] \exp\!\left(   \log\sigma_0^{(s)}   + \log\sigma_	ext{slope}^{(s)} \cdot \log\!\left|	ilde{y}^{(s,k)}ight| ight)   & 	ext{if } 	exttt{use\_heteroscedastic\_noise = TRUE}   \quad	ext{(CDAN)} \end{cases} $$  The homoscedastic path uses a constant noise SD drawn from the posterior ‚ÄĒ a valid posterior-predictive precision measure but one whose profile shape is driven mainly by the inverse-curve geometry.  The heteroscedastic (CDAN) path uses the power-of-mean variance function from O'Malley (2008) ¬ß2: noise scales with the predicted response level, so the precision profile captures both curve geometry *and* the fact that instrument noise is larger at high signal values. Both $\log\sigma_0^{(s)}$ and $\log\sigma_	ext{slope}^{(s)}$ are themselves drawn from the posterior, so their uncertainty is fully propagated.  The heavy tails of the Student-t distribution ($
u$ also posterior) provide a realistic characterisation of occasional outlier instrument readings in both modes.  The noisy response $y^{*(s,k)}$ is then **back-calculated** through the analytical inverse of the forward model:  $$ \hat{x}^{(s,k)} = f^{-1}\!\left(y^{*(s,k)};\;  oldsymbol{	heta}_k^{(s)}ight) $$  The collection $\left\{\hat{x}^{(s,k)}ight\}$ is the back-calculation distribution at $x^*$. The `pcov` column is the SD of this collection divided by the mean, expressed as a percentage (on the log scale: $	ext{pcov} = 	ext{SE} 	imes \ln(10) 	imes 100$). The `pcov_rmse` column is the relative RMSE against the known true grid point ‚ÄĒ the O'Malley (2008) CDAN precision metric when `use_heteroscedastic_noise = TRUE`.  The three-step logic can be illustrated concisely for both modes:   ``` r set.seed(123)  # Extract 400 posterior samples for logistic4 draws_mat <- posterior::as_draws_matrix(cmdstan_fit_4pl$draws()) idx       <- sample(nrow(draws_mat), 400) samp      <- draws_mat[idx, ]  # Plate-level parameters for curve_id = 1 A         <- as.numeric(samp[, "a[1]"]) B         <- as.numeric(samp[, "b[1]"]) C         <- as.numeric(samp[, "c_par[1]"]) D         <- as.numeric(samp[, "d[1]"]) sigma_obs <- as.numeric(samp[, "sigma_obs"]) nu        <- as.numeric(samp[, "nu"])  # Heteroscedastic noise parameters (always estimated, regardless of mode) log_sigma0     <- as.numeric(samp[, "log_sigma0"]) log_sigma_slope <- as.numeric(samp[, "log_sigma_slope"])  # Grid point: log10(1 AU/mL) = 0 x_star <- 0  # Step 1‚Äď2: forward prediction y_tilde <- curveRcore::logistic4(x_star, A, B, C, D)  # ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Mode 0: homoscedastic sigma_obs ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ sigma_homo <- sigma_obs y_star_homo <- y_tilde + sigma_homo * rt(length(y_tilde), df = median(nu)) x_hat_homo <- vapply(seq_along(A), function(s) {   tryCatch(     curveRcore::inv_logistic4(y_star_homo[s], A[s], B[s], C[s], D[s]),     error = function(e) NA_real_   ) }, numeric(1)) x_hat_homo <- x_hat_homo[is.finite(x_hat_homo)]  # ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Mode 1: heteroscedastic sigma_i = exp(log_sigma0 + log_sigma_slope * log|mu|) ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ sigma_hetero <- exp(log_sigma0 + log_sigma_slope * log(abs(y_tilde) + 1e-10)) y_star_hetero <- y_tilde + sigma_hetero * rt(length(y_tilde), df = median(nu)) x_hat_hetero <- vapply(seq_along(A), function(s) {   tryCatch(     curveRcore::inv_logistic4(y_star_hetero[s], A[s], B[s], C[s], D[s]),     error = function(e) NA_real_   ) }, numeric(1)) x_hat_hetero <- x_hat_hetero[is.finite(x_hat_hetero)]  cat(sprintf(   "Back-calculation at log10(x*) = %.1f  [x* = %.0f AU/mL]

",   x_star, 10^x_star )) #> Back-calculation at log10(x*) = 0.0  [x* = 1 AU/mL] cat("Mode 0 ‚ÄĒ homoscedastic (sigma_obs):
") #> Mode 0 ‚ÄĒ homoscedastic (sigma_obs): cat(sprintf("  Posterior mean:  %.4f  log10(AU/mL)
", mean(x_hat_homo))) #>   Posterior mean:  0.0053  log10(AU/mL) cat(sprintf("  Posterior SD:    %.4f  log10(AU/mL)
",   sd(x_hat_homo))) #>   Posterior SD:    0.1103  log10(AU/mL) cat(sprintf("  Back-calc CV:    %.1f%%

",             sd(x_hat_homo) * log(10) * 100)) #>   Back-calc CV:    25.4% cat("Mode 1 ‚ÄĒ heteroscedastic CDAN (sigma_i = exp(log_sigma0 + log_sigma_slope*log|mu|)):
") #> Mode 1 ‚ÄĒ heteroscedastic CDAN (sigma_i = exp(log_sigma0 + log_sigma_slope*log|mu|)): cat(sprintf("  Posterior mean:  %.4f  log10(AU/mL)
", mean(x_hat_hetero))) #>   Posterior mean:  -0.1573  log10(AU/mL) cat(sprintf("  Posterior SD:    %.4f  log10(AU/mL)
",   sd(x_hat_hetero))) #>   Posterior SD:    0.6142  log10(AU/mL) cat(sprintf("  Back-calc CV:    %.1f%%
",             sd(x_hat_hetero) * log(10) * 100)) #>   Back-calc CV:    141.4%

# Stack all per-curve standards into a single data frame stds_all <- collect_standards_bayes(fit) cat(sprintf("Standards collected: %d rows across %d curve_ids
",             nrow(stds_all), dplyr::n_distinct(stds_all$curve_id))) #> Standards collected: 60 rows across 6 curve_ids  # Stack all per-curve blanks (NULL if no blanks were provided) blanks_all <- collect_blanks_bayes(fit) if (!is.null(blanks_all)) {   cat(sprintf("Blanks collected:    %d rows across %d curve_ids

") } #> Blanks collected:    24 rows across 6 curve_ids # Verify per-plate standard and blank counts from the summary table summary_table_bayes(fit) |>   dplyr::select(curve_id, n_standards, n_blanks, noise_mode) |>   knitr::kable(caption = "Per-plate data coverage. n_blanks = 0 when no blanks were supplied.") ```{r qa-blanks-plot,     fig.cap="Blank well responses per curve_id. The horizontal line marks the posterior mean lower asymptote for each curve ‚ÄĒ blanks should cluster near it."} if (!is.null(blanks_all) && nrow(blanks_all) > 0) {    # Posterior mean lower asymptote per curve_id   a_means <- purrr::map_dfr(     names(fit$plates),     function(cid) {       cr   <- fit$plates[[cid]]       best <- cr$selection$best_model_name       p    <- cr$ensemble[[best]]$parameters       tibble::tibble(         curve_id = as.integer(cid),         a_mean   = if (!is.null(p) && "a" %in% p$term)                      p$mean[p$term == "a"]                    else NA_real_       )     }   )    blanks_all |>     dplyr::mutate(curve_id = as.integer(curve_id)) |>     ggplot(aes(x = factor(curve_id),                y = .data[[bead_assay_example$response_var]])) +     geom_jitter(width = 0.1, size = 2, colour = "firebrick", alpha = 0.8) +     geom_hline(       data = a_means,       aes(yintercept = a_mean, group = factor(curve_id)),       linetype = "dashed", colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.6     ) +     labs(       x     = "curve_id",       y     = "log₁₀(MFI)  [blank wells]",       title = "Blank well responses vs posterior mean lower asymptote"     ) +     theme_minimal(base_size = 12) }

title: curveRbayes 0.2.0
text: Initial release. fit_calibration_bayes() ‚ÄĒ hierarchical Bayesian calibration across one curve_id values simultaneously via Stan. Returns calibration_result_multiplate matching curveRfreq output contract. Five Stan model families: logistic4, logistic5, loglogistic4, loglogistic5, gompertz4 ‚ÄĒ non-centred parameterisation (NCP) reduce_sum map-reduce likelihood multi-core speedup. compile_stan_model() ‚ÄĒ compiles (caches) Stan model via cmdstanr. fit_bayes_single() ‚ÄĒ runs HMC/NUTS sampling one model family across curve_ids returns posterior draws plus NUTS diagnostics (divergences, max treedepth, E-BFMI). extract_curve_params() ‚ÄĒ extracts per-curve posterior summaries (mean, SD, 2.5/50/97.5 quantiles) fitted model object. build_stan_data() ‚ÄĒ converts preprocessed stacked standards named-list format expected Stan data {} block, including data-adaptive prior scalars. compute_dynamic_priors() ‚ÄĒ constructs weakly informative, data-adaptive hyperpriors preprocessed standards. Supports fixed_a soft constraint poorly-identified lower asymptotes. predict_grid_bayes() ‚ÄĒ CDAN (Concentration-Dependent Assay Noise) precision grid: three-step procedure posterior draw ‚Üí forward evaluation ‚Üí Student-t noise injection ‚Üí analytical back-calculation, producing pcov pcov_rmse precision profiles every grid point. predict_samples_bayes() ‚ÄĒ back-calculates test-sample concentrations posterior draws without noise injection (observed response already noisy measurement). Returns final_concentration, se_concentration, pcov, pcov_pass per sample. compute_loo() / compare_models_loo() ‚ÄĒ PSIS-LOO cross-validation Bayesian stacking weights via loo package. summary_table_bayes() / collect_samples_bayes() ‚ÄĒ tidy extraction calibration_result_multiplate objects (multiplate legacy single-curve formats supported). Two eligibility gates active Bayesian path: rel_se (posterior SD / |mean| per parameter) dynamic_range (log10 upper/lower asymptote ratio). at_bound vcov_condition gates bypassed (hard constraints; vcov matrix). Global eligibility: model must pass gates curve_ids eligible back-calculation. bead_assay_example synthetic dataset: two antigens √ó three plates, six curve_id values.
